title: Primary Aldosteronism in Resistant Hypertension
subtitle: Spironolactone-Confounded ARR · Washout Protocol · Triple-Phase CT & AVS
authors: Clinical case vignette — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: 10-year treatment-resistant hypertension with persistent hypokalemia despite five antihypertensive agents
pmh: Resistant hypertension × 10 years, worsening over past 18 months
Mild generalized muscle weakness and occasional cramps

caseDescription:
57-year-old woman with BP 168/104 mmHg on five antihypertensives including spironolactone 25 mg, BMI 26.4, grade II hypertensive retinopathy, K+ 3.1 mEq/L despite supplementation, Na+ 143, Cr 0.88 (eGFR >90). Phenotype — severe resistant HTN + unprovoked hypokalemia — carries high pre-test probability for primary aldosteronism (PA).

Clinical conundrum: spironolactone competitively blocks mineralocorticoid receptors, inducing compensatory surge in renin and aldosterone, masking baseline physiology and threatening false-negative or uninterpretable screening. Cannot abruptly stop antihypertensives — requires phased diagnostic approach.

Workflow: (1) immediate baseline PAC/PRA or DRC ratio on current regimen; (2) if inconclusive, 4–6 week medication washout/substitution with K+ repletion; (3) after biochemical confirmation, triple-phase adrenal CECT; (4) if unilateral surgery planned and age >40, adrenal vein sampling (AVS) for lateralization.
keyFindings: Resistant HTN (5 drugs) + persistent hypokalemia → high pre-test probability for PA
Spironolactone on board confounds ARR — suppressed renin despite MRA is strongly presumptive of PA
Biochemical screening precedes imaging — incidentalomas and bilateral IAH limit CT utility early
Washout: stop spironolactone/MRA; substitute verapamil SR, hydralazine, prazosin/doxazosin
Aggressive K+ repletion during washout — hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone → false-negative screen
After confirmed PA: triple-phase adrenal CT; AVS if unilateral surgery planned and age >40
finalDiagnosis: Teaching vignette — high pre-test probability primary aldosteronism; diagnostic workup in progress with spironolactone-confounded ARR
learningObjectives: Recognize resistant hypertension + hypokalemia as high-yield PA phenotype
Interpret ARR on spironolactone and when washout is required
Sequence biochemical confirmation before adrenal imaging
Execute safe 4–6 week medication substitution without losing BP control
Select triple-phase adrenal CT and indications for AVS lateralization

items.title: Clinical snapshot: resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, and spironolactone on board
items.content:
57-year-old female: BP 168/104 mmHg, HR 72, BMI 26.4, grade II hypertensive retinopathy.
10-year resistant HTN worsening over 18 months despite adherence; mild weakness/cramps.
K+ 3.1 mEq/L (low despite supplementation); Na+ 143; Cr 0.88 (eGFR >90); FPG 96.
Five drugs: telmisartan 80, amlodipine 10, chlorthalidone 25, metoprolol 50, spironolactone 25 mg OD.
PAC/PRA pending — high pre-test probability for PA with MRA confounding screening.
keyPoints: 5-drug resistant HTN + hypokalemia → high PA probability.
Immediate step: baseline PAC/PRA or DRC ratio.
items.reference: Patient Presentation: "severe resistant hypertension requiring five medications alongside unprovoked/persistent hypokalemia—carries a high pre-test probability for Primary Aldosteronism (PA)" and "her current regimen includes Spironolactone (Aldactone), a competitive mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist."

items.question: Why does this patient's phenotype carry high pre-test probability for primary aldosteronism?
items.answer: Severe resistant hypertension requiring five medications alongside unprovoked/persistent hypokalemia is the classic PA phenotype. Despite oral potassium supplementation, K+ remains 3.1 mEq/L — consistent with aldosterone-driven renal potassium wasting. Muscle weakness and cramps further support hypokalemia-mediated symptoms.
keyPoints: Resistant HTN + hypokalemia = classic PA screen.
High pre-test probability warrants formal workup.
items.reference: Clinical Dilemma & Discussion: "severe resistant hypertension requiring five medications alongside unprovoked/persistent hypokalemia—carries a high pre-test probability for Primary Aldosteronism (PA)."

items.question: Why does spironolactone threaten to yield false-negative or uninterpretable PA screening results?
items.answer: Spironolactone is a competitive mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist blocking aldosterone at the cortical collecting duct. This induces a compensatory massive surge in both renin (PRA/DRC) and aldosterone. The drug alters baseline RAAS physiology, masking autonomous aldosterone production and threatening false-negative or entirely uninterpretable screening results.
keyPoints: MRA blocks receptor → compensatory renin/aldosterone surge.
Cannot abruptly stop all antihypertensives safely.
items.reference: Clinical Dilemma & Discussion: "By blocking aldosterone receptors at the cortical collecting duct, spironolactone induces a compensatory, massive surge in both Renin (PRA/DRC) and Aldosterone. This alteration masks the baseline physiology, threatening to yield false-negative or entirely uninterpretable screening results."

items.question: Why must biochemical screening (ARR) precede adrenal CT imaging in this case?
items.answer: Approximately 10% of patients over age 50 harbor non-functioning adrenal incidentalomas — early CT may detect benign non-secreting nodules, leading to misdiagnosis and unnecessary high-risk surgery. Bilateral idiopathic adrenal hyperplasia (IAH), the most frequent PA cause in older adults, typically shows normal adrenal architecture on standard CT — a normal scan cannot rule out PA.
items.reference: Why Biochemical Screening Precedes Imaging: "Approximately 10% of patients over the age of 50 harbor non-functioning adrenal adenomas" and "Bilateral Idiopathic Adrenal Hyperplasia (IAH) is the most frequent cause of PA in older adults. IAH typically presents with completely normal adrenal architecture on a standard CT scan."

items.question: Why does suppressed renin on spironolactone provide strongly presumptive evidence of PA?
items.answer: Spironolactone competitively blocks mineralocorticoid receptors and should normally drive renin upward through compensatory feedback. If renin remains fully suppressed (PRA <1.0 ng/mL/h or low DRC) despite this MRA blockade, autonomous aldosterone production is overwhelming the feedback loop. Primary hyperaldosteronism is almost certainly present.
keyPoints: MRA should elevate renin — if it doesn't, autonomy is extreme.
PRA <1.0 ng/mL/h on spironolactone = highly presumptive PA.
Suppressed renin despite blockade = autonomous secretion.
May obviate immediate washout if pattern clear.
items.reference: Interpreting the ARR on Spironolactone: "If renin remains fully suppressed despite the competitive blockade of spironolactone (which should normally drive renin up), this provides strong, highly presumptive evidence of autonomous aldosterone production. Primary hyperaldosteronism is almost certainly present."

items.question: Why must serum potassium be aggressively corrected throughout the washout phase?
items.answer: Hypokalemia directly suppresses aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex. Even in confirmed PA, low K+ can cause false-negative screening results by blunting aldosterone output. Aggressive oral potassium supplementation throughout the entire washout phase is a crucial clinical safeguard to avoid missing the diagnosis.
keyPoints: False-negative ARR possible even in true PA.
Current K+ 3.1 — must correct before repeat testing.
items.reference: Crucial Clinical Safeguard: "Serum potassium must be aggressively corrected with oral potassium supplementation throughout this entire washout phase. Hypokalemia directly suppresses aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex, which can cause a false-negative screening result even in confirmed PA."

items.question: Why are verapamil SR, hydralazine, and prazosin/doxazosin preferred during PA washout?
items.answer: These agents have minimal footprint on the RAAS axis compared to ACE inhibitors, ARBs, beta-blockers, dihydropyridine CCBs, and MRAs — all of which dynamically alter renin dynamics and confound ARR interpretation. They permit blood pressure control during 4–6 week washout without compromising biochemical readability.
keyPoints: Verapamil SR — non-DHP CCB, minimal renin effect.
Maintain BP control without confounding ARR.
items.reference: Safe Permitted Medications: "Verapamil Slow-Release (Non-dihydropyridine Calcium Channel Blocker) • Hydralazine (Direct Vasodilator) • Prazosin / Doxazosin (Alpha-1 Adrenergic Receptor Antagonists)" and "Minimal Footprint on the RAAS Axis."

items.question: Why is triple-phase adrenal CT preferred over standard abdominal CECT for PA localization?
items.answer: Standard abdominal CT uses thicker slices and lacks precise contrast-transit timing for small adrenal structures. Dedicated triple-phase protocol (unenhanced, early contrast-enhanced, delayed washout) calculates absolute and relative contrast washout. Lipid-rich adenomas (Conn's) show rapid uptake then >60% absolute washout; lipid-poor masses (malignancy/pheochromocytoma) retain contrast longer.
keyPoints: Standard CT inadequate for small adrenal lesions.
Lipid-poor masses retain contrast — different pathology.
items.reference: Why Triple-Phase over Standard Abdominal CECT?: "Standard abdominal CT protocols utilize thicker slices and lack the precise contrast-transit timing needed to map small adrenal structures" and "Adrenal Adenomas (Conn's Syndrome): Typically lipid-rich lesions... rapid contrast clearance (> 60% absolute washout on delayed imaging)."

items.question: Why is adrenal vein sampling (AVS) indicated before unilateral adrenalectomy in patients over 40?
items.answer: After biochemical confirmation of PA, imaging identifies adrenal anatomy but cannot reliably lateralize aldosterone secretion — especially with bilateral disease or incidentalomas. If unilateral surgery is planned and patient is >40 years old, AVS confirms lateralization to avoid unnecessary or incorrect adrenalectomy.
keyPoints: CT alone may not lateralize secretion.
AVS confirms side of autonomous production.
Required before unilateral surgery if >40 years.
items.reference: Summary Clinical Workflow: "If unilateral surgery is planned (and patient is >40 years old), proceed to Adrenal Vein Sampling (AVS) to confirm lateralization."

items.question: How should the baseline ARR be interpreted when drawn on the current spironolactone-containing regimen?
items.answer: 1. Draw baseline PAC and PRA/DRC immediately — do not order CT first. 2. Interpret with high index of suspicion for drug interference. 3. If renin suppressed (PRA <1.0 ng/mL/h or low DRC) despite spironolactone → strongly presumptive PA. 4. If renin elevated/ambiguous → assay inconclusive; proceed to 4–6 week washout/substitution protocol and repeat ARR.
keyPoints: Suppressed renin on MRA = presumptive PA.
Look through lens of drug interference.
items.reference: Interpreting the ARR on Spironolactone: "The baseline panel must be interpreted with a high index of suspicion, looking through the lens of drug interference" and "If renin is elevated, the assay is inconclusive due to medication artifact."

items.question: How is the 4-to-6-week medication washout and substitution protocol executed safely?
items.answer: 1. Discontinue spironolactone/eplerenone/amiloride. 2. If clinically feasible, discontinue for 2 weeks: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, beta-blockers, dihydropyridine CCBs (e.g., amlodipine). 3. Substitute verapamil SR, hydralazine, and/or prazosin/doxazosin for BP control. 4. Aggressively replete potassium throughout. 5. Repeat ARR after washout period.
keyPoints: K+ repletion + repeat ARR at 4–6 weeks.
items.reference: 4-to-6-Week Substitution Regimen: "Discontinue: Spironolactone (Aldactone), Eplerenone, or Amiloride" and "Discontinue (if clinically feasible, for 2 weeks): ACE inhibitors, ARBs, Beta-blockers, and Dihydropyridine Calcium Channel Blockers."

items.question: How is the complete PA diagnostic workflow sequenced from presentation to localization?
items.answer: 1. Resistant HTN + hypokalemia → draw baseline PAC/PRA on current meds. 2a. Suppressed renin → strongly presumptive PA. 2b. Elevated/ambiguous renin → 4–6 week washout, K+ repletion, repeat ARR. 3. Biochemical confirmation (clean ARR ± confirmatory suppression test: saline infusion or oral sodium loading). 4. Triple-phase adrenal CECT. 5. If unilateral surgery planned and age >40 → AVS for lateralization.
keyPoints: CT then AVS if surgery candidate >40.
items.reference: Summary Clinical Workflow: "Draw Baseline PAC & PRA/DRC (On Current Regimen)" → "Biochemical Confirmation of PA" → "Order Triple-Phase Adrenal CT" → "proceed to Adrenal Vein Sampling (AVS) to confirm lateralization."

items.question: 57-year-old woman with resistant HTN on 5 drugs including spironolactone 25 mg, K+ 3.1 mEq/L. Best immediate next diagnostic step?
options: Triple-phase adrenal CT
Baseline plasma aldosterone and renin activity (ARR) on current regimen
Immediate discontinuation of all antihypertensives for 2 weeks
24-hour urine cortisol
items.explanation: Despite spironolactone confounding, immediate baseline ARR is correct first step. CT must not precede biochemical screening. Cannot abruptly stop all antihypertensives.
items.reference: Step 1: "the immediate best next step is to draw a baseline Aldosterone-to-Renin Ratio (ARR). Advanced imaging (CECT or Triple-Phase Adrenal CT) should not be performed at this stage."

items.question: Baseline ARR on spironolactone returns PRA 0.4 ng/mL/h (suppressed). Most appropriate interpretation?
options: Rules out primary aldosteronism — renin is low
Strongly presumptive of autonomous aldosterone production despite MRA blockade
Normal finding on spironolactone — no further workup
Indicates renal artery stenosis
items.explanation: Spironolactone should drive renin up. Persistently suppressed PRA (<1.0) despite MRA = autonomous aldosterone overwhelming feedback — PA almost certainly present.
items.reference: Interpreting the ARR on Spironolactone: "If renin remains fully suppressed despite the competitive blockade of spironolactone... this provides strong, highly presumptive evidence of autonomous aldosterone production."

items.question: Why should adrenal CT not be ordered as the first test in this 57-year-old with suspected PA?
options: CT is contraindicated in hypertension
Incidentalomas are common and bilateral IAH may have normal CT — imaging cannot rule out PA
CT always misses adenomas >2 cm
Patient is too young for adrenal imaging
items.explanation: ~10% have non-functioning incidentalomas (misdiagnosis risk). Bilateral IAH, most common PA cause in older adults, often CT-normal. Biochemistry must precede imaging.
items.reference: Why Biochemical Screening Precedes Imaging: "Approximately 10% of patients over the age of 50 harbor non-functioning adrenal adenomas" and "IAH typically presents with completely normal adrenal architecture on a standard CT scan."

items.question: ARR is inconclusive (elevated renin) on spironolactone. Which medication should be discontinued FIRST in the washout protocol?
options: Telmisartan only
Spironolactone (and other MRAs: eplerenone, amiloride)
Chlorthalidone only
All five medications immediately without substitution
items.explanation: Spironolactone/MRA is the primary ARR confounder inducing compensatory renin/aldosterone surge. Discontinue MRA first, then ACEi/ARB/BB/DHP-CCB if feasible, with RAAS-neutral substitutes.
items.reference: 4-to-6-Week Substitution Regimen: "Discontinue: Spironolactone (Aldactone), Eplerenone, or Amiloride."

items.question: During PA washout, which antihypertensive agents are preferred to maintain BP control with minimal RAAS interference?
options: Lisinopril and carvedilol
Amlodipine and metoprolol
Verapamil SR, hydralazine, and prazosin/doxazosin
Spironolactone and eplerenone
items.explanation: Verapamil SR (non-DHP CCB), hydralazine, and α₁-blockers have minimal RAAS footprint. ACEi/ARB/BB/DHP-CCB and MRAs alter renin dynamics.
items.reference: Safe Permitted Medications: "Verapamil Slow-Release... Hydralazine... Prazosin / Doxazosin" with "Minimal Footprint on the RAAS Axis."

items.question: Patient undergoing PA washout has K+ 3.0 mEq/L. Critical action before repeat ARR?
options: Defer K+ repletion until after ARR to avoid false positives
Aggressively replete potassium — hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone and causes false-negative screens
Start fludrocortisone to raise K+
Discontinue all potassium supplementation
items.explanation: Hypokalemia directly suppresses adrenal aldosterone secretion → false-negative ARR even in confirmed PA. Aggressive K+ repletion throughout washout is mandatory.
items.reference: Crucial Clinical Safeguard: "Hypokalemia directly suppresses aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex, which can cause a false-negative screening result even in confirmed PA."

items.question: After biochemical confirmation of PA, preferred imaging modality is:
options: Standard single-phase abdominal CT
Renal artery Doppler ultrasound
Dedicated triple-phase adrenal CT (unenhanced, early enhanced, delayed washout)
MRI brain
items.explanation: Triple-phase adrenal CT with washout calculations distinguishes lipid-rich adenomas (>60% absolute washout) from lipid-poor masses. Standard abdominal CT lacks timing precision.
items.reference: Step 3: "The imaging modality of choice is a dedicated Triple-Phase Adrenal CT, utilizing unenhanced, early contrast-enhanced, and delayed washout phases."

items.question: 58-year-old with confirmed PA, CT shows 1.2 cm left adrenal nodule, unilateral adrenalectomy planned. Essential next step?
options: Proceed directly to left adrenalectomy based on CT
Adrenal vein sampling to confirm lateralization before surgery
Repeat ARR only — no further testing
Start lifelong spironolactone without surgery
items.explanation: If unilateral surgery planned and patient >40, AVS confirms lateralization. CT anatomy alone may not match functional secretion, especially with bilateral disease or incidentalomas.
items.reference: Summary Clinical Workflow: "If unilateral surgery is planned (and patient is >40 years old), proceed to Adrenal Vein Sampling (AVS) to confirm lateralization."

items.question: Which current medication in this patient's regimen is the PRIMARY confounder of ARR interpretation?
options: Chlorthalidone 25 mg
Telmisartan 80 mg
Spironolactone 25 mg
Metoprolol 50 mg
items.explanation: Spironolactone blocks mineralocorticoid receptors and induces compensatory renin/aldosterone surge — the primary MRA confounder. ARBs and BBs also alter renin but MRA effect is most distinctive.
items.reference: Clinical Dilemma: "her current regimen includes Spironolactone (Aldactone), a competitive mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist" that "masks the baseline physiology."

items.question: On triple-phase adrenal CT, a lipid-rich Conn's adenoma typically demonstrates:
options: Delayed contrast retention with <20% washout
Rapid early uptake followed by >60% absolute contrast washout on delayed imaging
No contrast uptake in any phase
Identical appearance to pheochromocytoma
items.explanation: Lipid-rich adenomas show rapid early enhancement then rapid clearance (>60% absolute washout). Lipid-poor masses retain contrast — malignancy/pheochromocytoma pattern.
items.reference: Why Triple-Phase over Standard Abdominal CECT?: "Adrenal Adenomas (Conn's Syndrome): Typically lipid-rich lesions. They demonstrate rapid contrast uptake during early phases followed by rapid contrast clearance (> 60% absolute washout on delayed imaging)."

items.statement: Triple-phase adrenal CT should be the first diagnostic test in suspected primary aldosteronism.
items.explanation: False. Baseline ARR precedes imaging. Early CT risks incidentaloma misdiagnosis and cannot exclude bilateral IAH.
items.reference: Step 1: "Advanced imaging (CECT or Triple-Phase Adrenal CT) should not be performed at this stage."

items.statement: Spironolactone typically causes a compensatory increase in both plasma renin and aldosterone levels.
items.explanation: True. MRA blockade at collecting duct induces compensatory surge in renin and aldosterone, confounding ARR.
items.reference: Clinical Dilemma: "spironolactone induces a compensatory, massive surge in both Renin (PRA/DRC) and Aldosterone."

items.statement: A normal adrenal CT scan can reliably rule out primary aldosteronism.
items.explanation: False. Bilateral IAH, most common PA cause in older adults, often has normal CT architecture.
items.reference: Why Biochemical Screening Precedes Imaging: "a normal scan cannot rule out the diagnosis."

items.statement: Hypokalemia during PA screening can produce a false-negative aldosterone result.
items.explanation: True. Low K+ directly suppresses adrenal aldosterone secretion even in confirmed PA.
items.reference: Crucial Clinical Safeguard: "Hypokalemia directly suppresses aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex, which can cause a false-negative screening result even in confirmed PA."

items.statement: If renin is elevated on spironolactone, the ARR is inconclusive and washout is required.
items.explanation: True. Elevated renin on MRA suggests medication artifact — proceed to 4–6 week substitution and repeat ARR.
items.reference: Interpreting the ARR on Spironolactone: "If renin is elevated, the assay is inconclusive due to medication artifact. The patient must then transition to a washout/substitution protocol."

items.assertion: Baseline aldosterone-to-renin ratio should be drawn immediately on the patient's current medication regimen.
items.reason: It is clinically unsafe to abruptly discontinue her antihypertensive regimen, yet screening must begin despite spironolactone confounding.
items.explanation: Both true. ARR on current meds is first step; interpretation accounts for spironolactone interference; washout only if inconclusive.
items.reference: Step 1: "the immediate best next step is to draw a baseline Aldosterone-to-Renin Ratio (ARR)" and "Because it is clinically unsafe to abruptly discontinue her anti-hypertensive regimen."

items.assertion: Serum potassium must be aggressively repleted throughout the PA washout period.
items.reason: Hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex and can cause false-negative screening.
items.explanation: Both true; hypokalemia is both a clinical problem in this patient (K+ 3.1) and a biochemical trap for PA diagnosis.
items.reference: Crucial Clinical Safeguard: "Serum potassium must be aggressively corrected with oral potassium supplementation throughout this entire washout phase" and "Hypokalemia directly suppresses aldosterone secretion."

items.stem: Which clinical findings most strongly suggest primary aldosteronism as the cause of this patient's hypertension?
options: A) Severe resistant hypertension requiring multiple medications and hypokalemia unresponsive to oral supplementation
B) Pheochromocytoma with episodic hypertension and headaches
C) Cushing's syndrome with central obesity and glucose intolerance
D) Renal artery stenosis confirmed by angiography
items.explanation: Resistant hypertension combined with hypokalemia refractory to supplementation is the classic phenotype of primary aldosteronism, reflecting aldosterone-mediated renal potassium wasting.

items.assertion: Spironolactone use can lead to false-negative results in primary aldosteronism (PA) screening.
items.reason: Spironolactone induces a compensatory surge in renin and aldosterone, masking autonomous aldosterone production.
items.explanation: Spironolactone's mineralocorticoid receptor antagonism triggers RAAS activation, altering baseline physiology and obscuring true aldosterone excess in PA screening.

items.stem: Why should biochemical screening (aldosterone-renin ratio) precede adrenal CT imaging in a patient with suspected primary aldosteronism?
options: A) To avoid radiation exposure from CT scans
B) To identify adrenal incidentalomas that may require surgical removal
C) To prevent misdiagnosis of non-functioning adrenal nodules and unnecessary surgery
D) To confirm the presence of adrenal adenomas before imaging
items.explanation: Approximately 10% of patients over age 50 have non-functioning adrenal incidentalomas that may appear normal on CT, leading to potential misdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery. Bilateral idiopathic adrenal hyperplasia (the most common cause of PA in older adults) often shows normal adrenal architecture on CT, making biochemical confirmation essential before anatomical imaging.

items.stem: Why is aggressive correction of serum potassium essential during the washout phase for diagnosing primary aldosteronism (PA)?
options: A) To prevent cardiac arrhythmias caused by hypokalemia
B) To avoid false-negative aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR) results due to aldosterone suppression
C) To normalize renal function impaired by chronic hypokalemia
D) To mitigate metabolic alkalosis associated with aldosteronism
items.explanation: Hypokalemia suppresses adrenal aldosterone secretion, which can blunt aldosterone output even in PA, leading to falsely low ARR values. Maintaining potassium levels prevents this diagnostic pitfall.

items.stem: Why are verapamil SR, hydralazine, and prazosin/doxazosin preferred during PA washout?
options: A) These agents have minimal effect on the RAAS axis, preserving biochemical readability of ARR without altering renin dynamics.
B) They directly inhibit aldosterone production, reducing false positives in ARR testing.
C) They are more effective than ACE inhibitors in lowering blood pressure during washout.
D) Their use prevents the need for prolonged washout periods by stabilizing renin levels.
items.explanation: Verapamil SR (non-DHP CCB), hydralazine (direct vasodilator), and prazosin/doxazosin (α₁ blockers) minimally affect RAAS, unlike ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and MRAs, which alter renin dynamics and confound ARR interpretation. This allows blood pressure control during washout without compromising biochemical accuracy.

items.stem: Why is triple-phase adrenal CT preferred over standard abdominal CECT for adrenal lesion characterization?
options: A. Standard CT provides higher spatial resolution for small adrenal lesions
B. Triple-phase protocol enables assessment of contrast washout patterns to differentiate adrenal adenomas from malignant or lipid-poor lesions
C. Standard CT uses lower radiation doses compared to triple-phase protocols
D. Triple-phase imaging is more cost-effective for adrenal lesion evaluation
items.explanation: Triple-phase adrenal CT (unenhanced, early, delayed) allows quantification of contrast washout, critical for distinguishing lipid-rich adenomas (>60% absolute washout) from lipid-poor lesions (malignancy/pheochromocytoma). Standard CT lacks this capability due to thicker slices and suboptimal contrast timing.

items.stem: Why is adrenal vein sampling (AVS) indicated before unilateral adrenalectomy in patients over 40 with confirmed primary aldosteronism (PA)?
options: A) To confirm the diagnosis of PA
B) To assess adrenal tumor size
C) To lateralize aldosterone secretion when imaging is inconclusive
D) To monitor response to medical therapy
items.explanation: CT imaging may not reliably lateralize aldosterone secretion, especially in bilateral disease or incidentalomas. AVS confirms the side of autonomous production to guide appropriate unilateral surgery and avoid unnecessary or incorrect adrenalectomy in patients >40 years.

items.stem: When interpreting baseline aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR) on a spironolactone-containing regimen, which statement is correct?
options: A) Order CT scan first before measuring PAC/PRA
B) Suppressed renin (PRA <1.0 ng/mL/h) confirms adrenal adenoma
C) Elevated renin levels require immediate adrenal venous sampling
D) Suppressed renin despite spironolactone suggests strong presumptive primary aldosteronism
items.explanation: Spironolactone suppresses renin; suppressed renin in this context strongly suggests primary aldosteronism. Elevated renin requires washout, not immediate imaging. CT should not be ordered before ARR interpretation.

items.stem: Which of the following correctly describes the 4-to-6-week medication washout and substitution protocol for safe execution?
options: A. Discontinue MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone/amiloride) first; substitute RAAS-neutral antihypertensives (verapamil SR, hydralazine, prazosin/doxazosin); aggressively replete potassium; repeat ARR after washout.
B. Continue ACEi/ARB while discontinuing MRA; substitute DHP-CCB (amlodipine); avoid potassium repletion.
C. Discontinue beta-blockers for 2 weeks; substitute ACEi/ARB; omit potassium repletion.
D. Discontinue MRA and DHP-CCB simultaneously; substitute thiazide diuretic; repeat ARR without potassium repletion.
items.explanation: Correct answer (A) aligns with key steps: MRA discontinuation first (primary confounder), RAAS-neutral substitution (verapamil SR, hydralazine, prazosin/doxazosin), potassium repletion, and ARR repetition. Distractors (B-D) incorrectly retain or substitute non-RAAS-neutral agents, omit potassium repletion, or use inappropriate diuretics.

items.stem: What is the correct sequence for the complete PA diagnostic workflow from presentation to localization?
options: A. Resistant HTN + hypokalemia → baseline PAC/PRA → suppressed renin → biochemical confirmation → CT → AVS (if surgery candidate >40)
B. Resistant HTN + hypokalemia → CT → baseline PAC/PRA → washout → ARR → AVS
C. Resistant HTN + hypokalemia → ARR → elevated renin → CT → biochemical confirmation → AVS
D. Resistant HTN + hypokalemia → biochemical confirmation → washout → CT → AVS (if surgery candidate >40)
items.explanation: Biochemistry (PAC/PRA and ARR) must precede imaging (CT). Washout and confirmatory tests (saline infusion) occur after an inconclusive ARR. AVS is reserved for unilateral surgery candidates over 40.

items.assertion: Suppressed renin levels while on spironolactone strongly suggest primary hyperaldosteronism (PA).
items.reason: Spironolactone normally increases renin by blocking mineralocorticoid receptors, so suppression indicates autonomous aldosterone production.
items.explanation: The reasoning links the suppression of renin to autonomous aldosterone production in PA.
